title: LC-MS/MS 13-Steroid Panel
subtitle: Glass-Box Adrenal & Gonadal Steroidogenesis — CAH Variants, PCOS, AME, ACC & Orphan Defects

items.title: Why does the LC-MS/MS 13-steroid panel provide a glass-box view of steroidogenesis?
items.content:
Tracks exact substrate-to-product flux across Δ⁴ and Δ⁵ pathways simultaneously.
Maps enzymatic bottlenecks mathematically — not isolated markers in isolation.
LC-MS/MS cutoffs run significantly lower and tighter than legacy immunoassay reference ranges.
Measures accumulated precursors and deficient downstream products in the same specimen.
keyPoints: Definitive precursor + product mapping in one panel.
items.reference: Panel Overview: "By tracking the exact substrate-to-product flux across the Δ⁴ and Δ⁵ pathways, this panel moves beyond isolated markers to map enzymatic bottlenecks mathematically" and "utilizing LC-MS/MS-specific cutoffs (which run significantly lower and tighter than legacy immunoassay reference ranges)."

items.title: Why does 21-deoxycortisol make LC-MS/MS definitively diagnostic for 21-OHD?
items.content:
21-Deoxycortisol inclusion circumvents cross-reactivity and false-positive issues inherent to standard 17-OHP immunoassays.
Classic 21-OHD: massive elevations in 17-OHP, 21-deoxycortisol, androstenedione, testosterone; suppressed cortisol and aldosterone.
NCCAH: moderately elevated 17-OHP and 21-deoxycortisol with hyperandrogenemia.
Basal LC-MS/MS: 17-OHP >200 ng/dL (6 nmol/L) mandates Synacthen stimulation; 21-deoxycortisol >10 ng/dL (0.31 nmol/L) near 100% specificity for carrier/NCCAH.
keyPoints: Basal 21-deoxycortisol >10 ng/dL: high specificity for NCCAH.
items.reference: 21-Hydroxylase Deficiency: "The inclusion of 21-Deoxycortisol makes this panel definitively diagnostic for 21-OHD, completely circumventing the cross-reactivity and false-positive issues inherent to standard 17-OHP immunoassays" and "21-Deoxycortisol: > 10 ng/dL (0.31 nmol/L) provides near 100% specificity for carrier/NCCAH status."

items.title: What are the LC-MS/MS stimulated cutoffs for NCCAH — and why not use legacy RIA thresholds?
items.content:
Stimulated 17-OHP peak >780 ng/dL (23.4 nmol/L) confirms NCCAH on LC-MS/MS.
Stimulated 21-deoxycortisol peak >450 ng/dL (13.3 nmol/L).
Do NOT use legacy RIA/ELISA cutoff of 1000 ng/dL for stimulated 17-OHP — yields false negatives on LC-MS/MS.
Assay platform determines diagnostic thresholds.
keyPoints: Legacy 1000 ng/dL RIA cutoff → false negatives on LC-MS/MS.
items.reference: LC-MS/MS Cutoffs (Stimulated): "17-OHP: Peak > 780 ng/dL (23.4 nmol/L) confirms NCCAH. (Note: Do not use the legacy RIA/ELISA cutoff of 1000 ng/dL, which yields false negatives on LC-MS/MS)." and "21-Deoxycortisol: Peak > 450 ng/dL (13.3 nmol/L)."

items.title: How does 11β-hydroxylase deficiency (CYP11B1) present on the 13-steroid panel?
items.content:
Often misdiagnosed as essential hypertension with hyperandrogenism.
Marked elevation of 11-deoxycortisol (Compound S) and 11-deoxycorticosterone (DOC) — DOC acts as potent mineralocorticoid.
Cortisol low/normal; aldosterone suppressed due to DOC-driven volume expansion.
Cutoffs: 11-deoxycortisol basal >200 ng/dL, stimulated >2000 ng/dL (normal <100 ng/dL); DOC >30 ng/dL (adult normal usually <10 ng/dL).
items.reference: 11β-Hydroxylase Deficiency: "Marked elevation of 11-Deoxycortisol (Compound S) and 11-Deoxycorticosterone (DOC), which acts as a potent mineralocorticoid. Cortisol is low/normal; Aldosterone is suppressed due to DOC-driven volume expansion" and "11-Deoxycortisol: Basal > 200 ng/dL; Stimulated > 2000 ng/dL."

items.title: What is the steroid signature of CYP17A1 (17α-hydroxylase/17,20-lyase) deficiency?
items.content:
Presents as primary amenorrhea, sexual infantilism, hypertension.
Pathway shunted entirely leftward into mineralocorticoids.
Elevated: progesterone, DOC, corticosterone.
Suppressed hallmarks: near-zero 17-OHP, 11-deoxycortisol, cortisol, and all androgens (DHEA, DHEAS, androstenedione, testosterone).
Cutoffs: DHEAS <15 µg/dL alongside elevated DOC >30 ng/dL.
items.reference: 17α-Hydroxylase/17,20-Lyase Deficiency: "Suppressed (Diagnostic Hallmarks): Near-zero levels of 17-OHP, 11-Deoxycortisol, Cortisol, and all androgens" and "Cutoffs: DHEAS < 15 µg/dL alongside elevated DOC > 30 ng/dL."

items.title: How is 3β-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase type 2 deficiency diagnosed via Δ⁵/Δ⁴ ratios?
items.content:
Blockade between Δ⁵ and Δ⁴ steroids.
Elevated DHEA, DHEAS, 17-OHP but impaired conversion to androstenedione and progesterone.
Diagnostic ratio = Δ⁵ steroids / Δ⁴ steroids.
Markedly elevated 17-OHP/progesterone and DHEA/androstenedione ratios confirm defect.
keyPoints: Δ⁵ accumulation with impaired Δ⁴ conversion.
Not diagnosed by single analyte alone.
Orphan CAH variant captured by flux mapping.
items.reference: 3β-Hydroxysteroid Dehydrogenase Type 2 Deficiency: "Diagnosis via Ratio: Diagnostic Ratio = Δ⁵ Steroids / Δ⁴ Steroids. Markedly elevated 17-OHP/Progesterone and DHEA/Androstenedione ratios confirm the defect."

items.title: How does the panel differentiate PCOS ovarian hyperandrogenism from adrenal and neoplastic sources?
items.content:
PCOS (ovarian): elevated testosterone and androstenedione; normal DHEAS and cortisol. Ultrasensitive testosterone 50–150 ng/dL.
Adrenal hyperandrogenism: elevated DHEAS alongside androstenedione; DHEAS >350 µg/dL (age-dependent).
Virilizing tumor: massive solitary androgen spikes — testosterone >150 ng/dL or DHEAS >700 µg/dL mandates immediate pelvic/adrenal imaging.
Crucial: normal/borderline 17-OHP and 21-deoxycortisol definitively rules out NCCAH as underlying PCOS-like etiology.
keyPoints: Tumor: T >150 or DHEAS >700 → urgent imaging.
items.reference: PCOS & Hyperandrogenism: "Normal/borderline 17-OHP and 21-Deoxycortisol on this panel definitively rules out NCCAH as the underlying etiology of the PCOS-like phenotype" and "Testosterone > 150 ng/dL or DHEAS > 700 µg/dL mandates immediate pelvic/adrenal imaging."

items.title: How is apparent mineralocorticoid excess diagnosed using the cortisol/cortisone ratio?
items.content:
Defective 11β-HSD2 (or pharmacologic inhibition via licorice/azole antifungals) prevents conversion of active cortisol to inactive cortisone.
Cortisol inappropriately activates mineralocorticoid receptor.
Profile: low aldosterone, suppressed renin, normal DOC.
Diagnostic ratio = cortisol/cortisone. Normal ~1:1 to 2:1. Ratio >5:1 highly indicative of AME or 11β-HSD2 blockade.
keyPoints: Low aldosterone, suppressed renin, normal DOC.
items.reference: Apparent Mineralocorticoid Excess: "A normal ratio is approximately 1:1 to 2:1. A ratio > 5:1 is highly indicative of AME or 11β-HSD2 blockade."

items.title: How does adrenocortical carcinoma differ from benign Cushing adenoma on the steroid panel?
items.content:
ACC: dedifferentiated profile — pushes immature precursors into bloodstream rather than final products.
Pan-elevation of precursors: simultaneous spikes in 11-deoxycortisol, DHEAS, androstenedione, 17-OHP out of proportion to cortisol.
Benign Cushing adenoma: typically only elevated cortisol with suppressed DHEAS and precursors.
keyPoints: ACC: precursor pan-elevation disproportionate to cortisol.
DHEAS suppressed in benign cortisol-secreting adenoma.
items.reference: Adrenocortical Carcinoma vs. Benign Adenoma: "ACC typically presents with a 'dedifferentiated' steroid profile, pushing immature precursors into the bloodstream rather than final products" and "A benign Cushing's adenoma will typically only show elevated Cortisol with suppressed DHEAS and precursors."

items.title: How does primary aldosteronism differ from DOC-producing tumors on this panel?
items.content:
Primary aldosteronism: elevated aldosterone (>15 ng/dL) with suppressed renin; DOC and corticosterone remain normal.
DOC-producing tumor: suppressed aldosterone but mineralocorticoid hypertension driven by isolated 11-deoxycorticosterone (>30–50 ng/dL) and corticosterone elevations.
Panel separates aldosterone-driven from DOC-driven mineralocorticoid excess.
keyPoints: Both present with hypertension + hypokalemia pattern.
items.reference: Primary Aldosteronism vs. DOC-Producing Tumors: "Primary Aldosteronism: Elevated Aldosterone (> 15 ng/dL) with suppressed renin. DOC and Corticosterone remain normal" and "DOC-Producing Tumor: Suppressed Aldosterone but marked mineralocorticoid hypertension driven entirely by isolated 11-Deoxycorticosterone (> 30-50 ng/dL)."

items.title: How does ectopic ACTH syndrome differ from pituitary Cushing's on precursor overspill?
items.content:
Massive ACTH from neuroendocrine tumors overwhelms adrenal enzyme capacity → relative functional blockades (especially 11β-hydroxylase).
Pituitary Cushing's rarely produces enough ACTH for massive overspill.
EAS: hypokalemic alkalosis driven by massive intermediate precursors, not just cortisol.
EAS overspill: simultaneous massive ↑11-deoxycortisol (>300 ng/dL) and DOC (>50 ng/dL) with very high cortisol; DHEAS typically elevated.
Pituitary: cortisol high but 11-deoxycortisol and DOC normal or only slightly elevated.
keyPoints: Pituitary: cortisol ↑ alone; precursors near normal.
Precursor overspill discriminates EAS from Cushing disease.
items.reference: Ectopic ACTH Syndrome vs. Pituitary Cushing's Disease: "EAS Diagnostic Overspill: Simultaneous massive elevations of 11-Deoxycortisol (> 300 ng/dL) and 11-Deoxycorticosterone (DOC) (> 50 ng/dL) alongside very high Cortisol" and "Pituitary Cushing's: Cortisol is high, but 11-Deoxycortisol and DOC remain within normal or only slightly elevated ranges."

items.title: What is the diagnostic signature of generalized glucocorticoid resistance (Chrousos syndrome)?
items.content:
Inactivating glucocorticoid receptor mutation — HPA axis lacks negative feedback → massive ACTH secretion.
Patients lack clinical Cushing features (receptor resistance) but present with severe mineralocorticoid hypertension and hyperandrogenism.
ACTH chronically drives unobstructed Δ⁴ and Δ⁵ pathways.
Signature: high cortisol (>25 µg/dL without diurnal rhythm) + marked ↑DOC, corticosterone, androstenedione, DHEAS.
keyPoints: GR mutation — feedback failure not cortisol excess toxicity.
items.reference: Generalized Glucocorticoid Resistance: "High Cortisol (> 25 µg/dL without diurnal rhythm) paired with marked elevations in DOC, Corticosterone, Androstenedione, and DHEAS."

items.title: How does P450 oxidoreductase (POR) deficiency mimic a confusing mix of 21-OHD and 17-OHD?
items.content:
POR provides electrons to CYP17A1 and CYP21A2 — mutation creates partial combined defect.
17,20-lyase activity of CYP17A1 exquisitely sensitive to POR deficiency.
Profile: 17-OHP and progesterone elevated (mimics 21-OHD) BUT androstenedione, DHEA, testosterone completely suppressed (unlike 21-OHD where massive).
Cortisol often low/normal. Antley-Bixler phenotype.
keyPoints: Combined partial CYP21A2 + CYP17A1 defect via POR.
items.reference: P450 Oxidoreductase (POR) Deficiency: "17-OHP and Progesterone are elevated (mimicking 21-OHD), but the crucial differential is that Androstenedione, DHEA, and Testosterone are completely suppressed (unlike 21-OHD, where they are massive)."

items.title: How is aldosterone synthase deficiency (CYP11B2) identified on this panel?
items.content:
Final step of mineralocorticoid pathway blocked → salt wasting, FTT in infancy.
Diagnostic block ratio = corticosterone/aldosterone.
Markedly elevated corticosterone with unmeasurable/suppressed aldosterone (<3 ng/dL) confirms terminal CYP11B2 defect.
Type I vs II differentiation requires 18-hydroxycorticosterone (absent from this panel) but general blockade is evident.
items.reference: Aldosterone Synthase Deficiency: "Diagnostic Block = Corticosterone / Aldosterone. Markedly elevated Corticosterone coupled with unmeasurable/suppressed Aldosterone (< 3 ng/dL) confirms the terminal defect."

items.title: How does the panel differentiate primary from secondary adrenal insufficiency?
items.content:
Primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison's): global cortex destruction — cortisol, aldosterone, DHEAS all suppressed. Precursors (17-OHP, 11-deoxycortisol) fail to rise during SST.
Secondary adrenal insufficiency (pituitary): profound ↓cortisol and ↓DHEAS from ACTH deficiency. Zona glomerulosa governed by RAAS not ACTH — aldosterone and corticosterone preserved (>5 ng/dL).
Mineralocorticoid preservation distinguishes SAI from PAI.
keyPoints: RAAS maintains zona glomerulosa in SAI.
items.reference: Primary vs. Secondary Adrenal Insufficiency: "Secondary Adrenal Insufficiency: ACTH deficiency causes profound drops in Cortisol and DHEAS. However, because the zona glomerulosa is governed by RAAS (not ACTH), Aldosterone and Corticosterone remain preserved (> 5 ng/dL)."

items.title: How does the panel differentiate premature adrenarche from CPP or virilizing tumors?
items.content:
Premature adrenarche: isolated zona reticularis maturation.
DHEAS rises to pubertal levels (40–130 µg/dL); androstenedione measurable.
Total testosterone remains strictly prepubertal (<10 ng/dL).
Testosterone >10 ng/dL invalidates benign PA — investigate CPP or tumors.
keyPoints: PA: ↑DHEAS, measurable A4, prepubertal T (<10 ng/dL).
Isolated adrenal androgen rise without gonadal activation.
items.reference: Premature Adrenarche: "DHEAS rises to pubertal levels (40 to 130 µg/dL) and Androstenedione becomes measurable, but Total Testosterone remains strictly prepubertal (< 10 ng/dL). A testosterone level > 10 ng/dL invalidates benign PA."

items.title: What progesterone cutoffs on LC-MS/MS assess ovulation and luteal phase adequacy?
items.content:
Mid-luteal corpus luteum assessment (days 21–23 standard cycle).
Progesterone <3 ng/dL: confirms anovulation.
3–10 ng/dL: luteal phase defect (inadequate corpus luteum).
>10 ng/dL: robust ovulatory cycle.
items.reference: Luteal Phase Defect / Anovulation: "A Progesterone level < 3 ng/dL during days 21-23 of a standard cycle confirms anovulation. A level between 3 and 10 ng/dL suggests a luteal phase defect... while > 10 ng/dL confirms a robust, ovulatory cycle."

items.question: On LC-MS/MS, basal 17-OHP is 250 ng/dL and 21-deoxycortisol is 14 ng/dL in a hyperandrogenic woman. Next step?
options: Diagnose Classic CAH and start hydrocortisone immediately without further testing
Basal 17-OHP >200 ng/dL mandates Synacthen stimulation; 21-deoxycortisol >10 ng/dL supports NCCAH/carrier workup
Use legacy stimulated cutoff 1000 ng/dL on LC-MS/MS
items.explanation: LC-MS/MS basal 17-OHP >200 ng/dL → SST. 21-deoxycortisol >10 ng/dL high specificity for NCCAH. Do not use legacy 1000 ng/dL RIA stimulated cutoff on LC-MS/MS.
items.reference: LC-MS/MS Cutoffs (Basal): "17-OHP: > 200 ng/dL (6 nmol/L) mandates a Synacthen (SST) stimulation test" and "21-Deoxycortisol: > 10 ng/dL (0.31 nmol/L) provides near 100% specificity for carrier/NCCAH status."

items.question: Post-Synacthen LC-MS/MS shows 17-OHP peak 850 ng/dL. Using LC-MS/MS-specific criteria, this confirms:
options: Excludes NCCAH — need >1000 ng/dL per standard RIA cutoff
Confirms NCCAH — LC-MS/MS stimulated peak >780 ng/dL
items.explanation: LC-MS/MS stimulated 17-OHP peak >780 ng/dL confirms NCCAH. Legacy RIA 1000 ng/dL cutoff yields false negatives on LC-MS/MS.
items.reference: LC-MS/MS Cutoffs (Stimulated): "17-OHP: Peak > 780 ng/dL (23.4 nmol/L) confirms NCCAH. (Note: Do not use the legacy RIA/ELISA cutoff of 1000 ng/dL, which yields false negatives on LC-MS/MS)."

items.question: Young woman with hypertension, hirsutism: 11-deoxycortisol 450 ng/dL basal, DOC 45 ng/dL, suppressed aldosterone. Diagnosis?
items.explanation: ↑11-deoxycortisol + ↑DOC with suppressed aldosterone = CYP11B1 block. DOC acts as potent mineralocorticoid causing hypertension. Often misdiagnosed as essential HTN.
items.reference: 11β-Hydroxylase Deficiency: "Marked elevation of 11-Deoxycortisol (Compound S) and 11-Deoxycorticosterone (DOC)... Aldosterone is suppressed due to DOC-driven volume expansion."

items.question: 46,XY adolescent with hypertension, sexual infantilism: DHEAS 8 µg/dL, DOC 55 ng/dL, near-zero testosterone and 17-OHP. Diagnosis?
options: CYP17A1 deficiency — mineralocorticoid shunting with suppressed androgens and glucocorticoid precursors
items.explanation: CYP17A1: ↑DOC, ↑corticosterone, near-zero 17-OHP and all androgens. DHEAS <15 µg/dL. Pathway shunted to mineralocorticoids.
items.reference: 17α-Hydroxylase/17,20-Lyase Deficiency: "Near-zero levels of 17-OHP, 11-Deoxycortisol, Cortisol, and all androgens" and "DHEAS < 15 µg/dL alongside elevated DOC > 30 ng/dL."

items.question: Markedly elevated 17-OHP/progesterone and DHEA/androstenedione ratios with impaired Δ⁴ product formation suggest:
items.explanation: 3β-HSD2: Δ⁵ accumulation (DHEA, 17-OHP) with impaired Δ⁴ conversion (progesterone, androstenedione). Ratio diagnosis essential.
items.reference: 3β-HSD2 Deficiency: "Markedly elevated 17-OHP/Progesterone and DHEA/Androstenedione ratios confirm the defect."

items.question: Hyperandrogenic woman: testosterone 95 ng/dL, androstenedione elevated, DHEAS normal, 17-OHP and 21-deoxycortisol borderline/normal. Most likely source?
options: Functional ovarian hyperandrogenism (PCOS) — normal 17-OHP/21-deoxycortisol excludes adrenal NCCAH
items.explanation: PCOS pattern: ↑T, ↑A4, normal DHEAS. Normal/borderline 17-OHP and 21-deoxycortisol rules out NCCAH as underlying etiology.
items.reference: PCOS & Hyperandrogenism: "Normal/borderline 17-OHP and 21-Deoxycortisol on this panel definitively rules out NCCAH as the underlying etiology of the PCOS-like phenotype."

items.question: Woman with testosterone 280 ng/dL and DHEAS 850 µg/dL. Immediate action?
options: Urgent pelvic and adrenal imaging — virilizing tumor thresholds exceeded
items.explanation: T >150 ng/dL or DHEAS >700 µg/dL = massive androgen spikes mandating immediate imaging for ovarian/adrenal neoplasm.
items.reference: Virilizing Tumor: "Testosterone > 150 ng/dL or DHEAS > 700 µg/dL mandates immediate pelvic/adrenal imaging."

items.question: Hypertensive patient: low aldosterone, suppressed renin, normal DOC, cortisol/cortisone ratio 8:1. Diagnosis?
items.explanation: AME: cortisol/cortisone >5:1 with low aldosterone, suppressed renin, normal DOC. Consider licorice or azole antifungal inhibition of 11β-HSD2.
items.reference: AME: "A ratio > 5:1 is highly indicative of AME or 11β-HSD2 blockade" and "Low Aldosterone, suppressed Renin, normal DOC."

items.question: Adrenal mass workup: cortisol modestly elevated but 11-deoxycortisol, DHEAS, androstenedione, and 17-OHP all massively elevated disproportionately. Suggests:
items.explanation: ACC dedifferentiated profile: precursor pan-elevation out of proportion to cortisol. Benign adenoma typically ↑cortisol only with suppressed DHEAS/precursors.
items.reference: ACC vs. Benign Adenoma: "Simultaneous spikes in 11-Deoxycortisol, DHEAS, Androstenedione, and 17-OHP out of proportion to Cortisol."

items.question: Patient with very high cortisol, 11-deoxycortisol 420 ng/dL, DOC 68 ng/dL, elevated DHEAS, hypokalemic alkalosis. Most likely source?
options: Pituitary Cushing disease — precursors remain normal
Ectopic ACTH syndrome — massive precursor overspill from ACTH overwhelming 11β-hydroxylase
items.explanation: EAS: massive ↑11-deoxycortisol (>300), ↑DOC (>50), ↑cortisol, ↑DHEAS. Pituitary Cushing's rarely causes this precursor overspill pattern.
items.reference: EAS Diagnostic Overspill: "Simultaneous massive elevations of 11-Deoxycortisol (> 300 ng/dL) and 11-Deoxycorticosterone (DOC) (> 50 ng/dL) alongside very high Cortisol."

items.question: Elevated 17-OHP and progesterone but completely suppressed androstenedione, DHEA, and testosterone. Cortisol low/normal. Diagnosis?
options: POR deficiency — mimics 21-OHD upstream but androgens suppressed unlike true 21-OHD
items.explanation: POR deficiency: ↑17-OHP/↑progesterone like 21-OHD BUT androgens completely suppressed (21-OHD has massive androgens). Combined partial CYP21A2/CYP17A1 defect.
items.reference: POR Deficiency: "17-OHP and Progesterone are elevated (mimicking 21-OHD), but... Androstenedione, DHEA, and Testosterone are completely suppressed (unlike 21-OHD, where they are massive)."

items.question: Low cortisol and low DHEAS but aldosterone 12 ng/dL and corticosterone preserved. Diagnosis?
options: Secondary adrenal insufficiency — zona glomerulosa preserved by RAAS
items.explanation: SAI: ↓cortisol, ↓DHEAS from ACTH loss; aldosterone/corticosterone preserved because zona glomerulosa is RAAS-driven not ACTH-dependent.
items.reference: Secondary Adrenal Insufficiency: "Aldosterone and Corticosterone remain preserved (> 5 ng/dL)."

items.question: 7-year-old with elevated DHEAS (85 µg/dL) and androstenedione but testosterone 8 ng/dL. Interpretation?
options: Virilizing adrenal tumor — urgent imaging mandatory
items.explanation: PA: pubertal DHEAS (40–130), measurable A4, but testosterone strictly prepubertal (<10 ng/dL). T >10 ng/dL would invalidate benign PA.
items.reference: Premature Adrenarche: "Total Testosterone remains strictly prepubertal (< 10 ng/dL). A testosterone level > 10 ng/dL invalidates benign PA."

items.question: Mid-luteal progesterone (day 22) is 5 ng/dL in infertility workup. Interpretation?
options: Luteal phase defect — progesterone 3–10 ng/dL suggests inadequate corpus luteum
items.explanation: P4 <3 = anovulation; 3–10 = luteal phase defect; >10 = robust ovulation. 5 ng/dL = inadequate luteal phase.
items.reference: Luteal Phase Defect: "A level between 3 and 10 ng/dL suggests a luteal phase defect (inadequate corpus luteum), while > 10 ng/dL confirms a robust, ovulatory cycle."

items.statement: The legacy stimulated 17-OHP cutoff of 1000 ng/dL from RIA/ELISA should be applied to LC-MS/MS results for NCCAH diagnosis.
items.explanation: False. LC-MS/MS stimulated peak >780 ng/dL confirms NCCAH. Legacy 1000 ng/dL RIA cutoff yields false negatives on LC-MS/MS.
items.reference: LC-MS/MS Cutoffs (Stimulated): "Do not use the legacy RIA/ELISA cutoff of 1000 ng/dL, which yields false negatives on LC-MS/MS."

items.statement: Normal 17-OHP and 21-deoxycortisol on LC-MS/MS rules out NCCAH as the cause of a PCOS-like hyperandrogenic phenotype.
items.explanation: True. Normal/borderline 17-OHP and 21-deoxycortisol definitively excludes NCCAH — supports ovarian functional hyperandrogenism workup.
items.reference: PCOS & Hyperandrogenism: "Normal/borderline 17-OHP and 21-Deoxycortisol on this panel definitively rules out NCCAH as the underlying etiology of the PCOS-like phenotype."

items.statement: A cortisol/cortisone ratio greater than 5:1 with low aldosterone and suppressed renin supports apparent mineralocorticoid excess.
items.explanation: True. Normal ratio ~1:1 to 2:1. >5:1 indicates 11β-HSD2 failure — AME or pharmacologic blockade.
items.reference: AME: "A ratio > 5:1 is highly indicative of AME or 11β-HSD2 blockade."

items.statement: POR deficiency presents with massive androgen elevation identical to Classic 21-OHD.
items.explanation: False. POR: ↑17-OHP mimics 21-OHD but androstenedione, DHEA, testosterone completely suppressed — key differential from 21-OHD.
items.reference: POR Deficiency: "Androstenedione, DHEA, and Testosterone are completely suppressed (unlike 21-OHD, where they are massive)."

items.assertion: 21-Deoxycortisol measurement on LC-MS/MS improves diagnostic specificity for 21-OHD over 17-OHP immunoassay alone.
items.reason: 21-Deoxycortisol avoids cross-reactivity and false-positive issues inherent to standard 17-OHP immunoassays.
items.explanation: Both true; 21-deoxycortisol is pathognomonic bypass product of severe CYP21A2 block with minimal immunoassay interference.
items.reference: 21-Hydroxylase Deficiency: "The inclusion of 21-Deoxycortisol makes this panel definitively diagnostic for 21-OHD, completely circumventing the cross-reactivity and false-positive issues inherent to standard 17-OHP immunoassays."

items.assertion: Secondary adrenal insufficiency can present with low cortisol but preserved aldosterone.
items.reason: The zona glomerulosa is regulated by RAAS rather than ACTH.
items.explanation: Both true; mineralocorticoid preservation is the key panel discriminator between PAI and SAI.
items.reference: Secondary Adrenal Insufficiency: "because the zona glomerulosa is governed by RAAS (not ACTH), Aldosterone and Corticosterone remain preserved (> 5 ng/dL)."
